taskDescription: Drag the correct anatomical terms into the blanks to complete these statements about the back of the leg and sole.
textField:
The calcaneal (Achilles) tendon is formed by the convergence of *gastrocnemius* and *soleus* tendons. It is the thickest and strongest tendon in the body.

The avascular zone of the calcaneal tendon lies *2-6* cm above the calcaneal insertion — this is where most ruptures occur.

The Thompson test involves squeezing the *calf* — if the foot does not plantarflex, the calcaneal tendon is ruptured.

The deep posterior compartment muscles passing behind the medial malleolus are remembered by the mnemonic 'Tom, Dick, ANd Very Nervous Harry': *tibialis posterior*, flexor digitorum longus, posterior tibial artery, tibial nerve, and *flexor hallucis longus*.

*Plantar fasciitis* is the most common cause of heel pain in adults.

The sole of the foot has four muscle layers. The first layer contains *abductor hallucis*, flexor digitorum brevis, and abductor digiti minimi.

The *medial* and *lateral* plantar nerves are terminal branches of the tibial nerve and supply the intrinsic muscles of the sole.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the posterior leg and sole anatomy and try again.
Good effort! Review the areas you missed.
Excellent! You have a strong grasp of posterior leg and sole anatomy.
